item.title: In memory of Guido Sohne - May 23, 2023
item.description: Guido Sohne, one of my heroes, would have turned 50 today. His remarkable achievements and dedication continue to inspire me. The author of the blog post titled ”For Guido, or Requiem for a True Tiger,” which you can find below, perfectly captures Guido’s impact, stating, “Here’s a guy who founded companies, co-founded NGOs, and advised international foundations, all with the noble purpose of bringing the promise of technology and innovation to those in his home who were less fortunate than he was.

item.title: Embassy and Proof of Funds
item.description: When the British Raj asked the Indians to kill poisonous cobras, it gave rise to the cobra effect. Proof of funds is another such policy as well. It has been completely hacked, at least in Nigeria, and I wouldn’t blame anyone who fools the British, Canadian, etc. governments in this way. It is the path of least resistance, and as we know, we are naturally configured to follow this path.

item.title: Fighting your own biochemistry
item.description: This is a losing battle. Our body’s biochemistry is incredibly powerful, making it extremely hard to resist its innate urges. Sooner or later, it gets what it wants. This is evident, for instance, in honey-trapping exploits, where people are seduced into compromising situations by attractive individuals against their better judgment. Even those with a high level of self-control can be susceptible to it, as our biochemistry can override our rational selves.

item.title: A short note on Polybius analysis of the Roman state
item.description: I recently read book 6 of Polybius’s histories, and it is uncanny how a tract written such a long time ago is still very relevant for state building, or better yet, human coordination at a national scale, in our modern time, most especially in the African context. Constitution and culture emanate from the need of men to coordinate, and the evolution from one form of them to another, as analyzed by Polybius, is an iterative attempt to arrive at the most optimal state.

item.title: Academic topics I hope will be researched in Africa
item.description: Hopefully, some of this already exists. If they do, please point me to them. Another question worth answering is how we can stimulate more rigorous academic research. How do we create an infrastructure that churns out scientific knowledge on a grand scale? What are the impacts of the traffic conditions on productivity and well-being in African states like Lagos? What are the effects of weaning a child from the left hand very early on?

item.title: Chronological age is a noisy aging signal
item.description: The traditional way of tracking age and, by extension, aging is to do a ++1 operation for each complete Gregorian year from the moment of birth. Socially, we even go out of our way to commemorate this, as if it were a good indicator of growing older or that the brutal reality of it is something worth celebrating [1]. No, it isn’t, and we should tweak this norm at scale. I think a good first step will be to recalibrate why and how we track age so that we can instead focus more purposefully on longevity [2].

item.title: My Thoughts On Balaji Srinivasan How To Start A New Country
item.description: I’ve been engrossed by the concept of a network state since reading an article recently read by Balaji S. Srinivasan on how to start a new country. As a Nigerian who, like many others, is barely surviving within a failing state and whose nationality is a byproduct of colonialism, the possibility that I and countless others can escape the Nigerian contraption in a pragmatic way and be part of a thriving, supranational state that transcends physical boundaries is irresistible, and here are my first thoughts:

item.title: On founding, founders and foundations
item.description: I just read the “Founding Brothers” and I now hold the founding fathers of the USA nation in higher esteem than previously. Their brilliance, dexterity, and foresight provided the bedrock for the American nation to thrive, and this is, incidentally, what most of the African nations severely lacked, unfortunately, at those auspicious founding moments post-independence. For those that had really capable and visionary founders e.g. Patrice Lumumba of Congo, their ingenuity was frustrated by the demands of the competing interests of the USA and USSR alongside neocolonialism.

item.title: Spotlighting people doing the lords work in Africa from my POV
item.description: This list is by no means all-encompassing, and I intend to keep updating it as I come across more. Gbenga Adewoyin Tayo Bello - Batoinspiration Esther Child Rights Foundation Ola0X Lalepa AI Ousman Touray Polytope Labs

item.title: The path of least resistance
item.description: I went to a friend’s place yesterday for Salah. He’s an old friend, and we have not hung out in a very long time since after university, so even though I’m naturally very reluctant to leave my house, I obliged him when he asked me to come over. It was an opportunity to catch up, to discuss the vicissitudes of life, what that has translated to in our individual lives, the opportunities that lie ahead of us, and most importantly, to pass this solemn, although implicit, pledge that we are together in this struggle to survive.

item.title: Best things I have seen online recently
item.description: I will keep updating this list. How to pick a co-founder Ideas are the new oil Advice for 10-20 year olds - Patrick Collison How to be successful - Sam Altman Exploit the weakness of thy neighbor: A rational reason to dislike pornography Violent enough to stand still Be kind - Andrew Bosworth Getting ahead - Charlie Munger Avoid ruin Age and the entrepreneur The four kinds of luck Cognitive biases in times of uncertainty

item.title: Dear Akin
item.description: I have been thinking about you, and having considered your antecedents and also the actions you have taken recently despite our numerous admonitions, I have decided to write this to you as a last recourse with the hope that it serves you well in the course of your life that is just beginning. Below, I highlighted 5 basic life principles, some of which I have had to learn the hard way, and even though they are by no means exhaustive, I firmly believe that if you adopt them, you will stand in good stead regardless of the circumstances at any point in your life onwards.

item.title: The meek shall not inherit Nigeria
item.description: In a world where politics and power struggles are constantly at play, one man dedicated his life to uncovering the secrets of cooperation and competition. Robert Axelrod was a brilliant political scientist whose groundbreaking research on the dynamics of human behavior has proven invaluable time and time again. In his experiments, Axelrod created a virtual game that pitted individuals against each other in a battle of self-interest versus cooperation. The rules were simple, yet the implications were profound: to win, players had to find the optimal balance between cooperation and competition.

item.title: XXVI
item.description: Sometimes, on your birthday, you can have the most profound awareness. For example, on 7/11/2012, which was in my second year at the university, I came to the conclusion that schooling at Unilorin was not just terrible but also irredeemable, and the prudent thing to do is to quit the system regardless of the sunk cost. To get in and out of the university campus, for instance, you’d have to spend several hours jostling for scarce taxis with most of your fellow students.

item.title: XXVII
item.description: Reflecting on the period between Nov 8, 2020 — November 8, 2021 It was a very tough year chai 😭. Apart from the days of running Token Nigeria, the middle of this period was probably the nadir of my life so far. Sometime between September 2020 and December 2020, I and Wisdom worked and iterated on the initial version of Bookmakr, an odds comparison tool that morphed into a booking code aggregation and conversion feed.
